claim.label: Replacement Pressure
claim.query: volunteer gaelic patriotic movement duty nation labour protestant unionist minority
claim.counter_query: protestant presbyterian minority labour civic municipal foreign travel commerce
claim.thesis: Track whether plurality and civic language are displaced by a more disciplined nationalist editorial vocabulary.

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XXIII NO. 24, JANUARY 27 1912
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
Home Rule,”” and we suppose this thing, such as it is,

has' been' exploited in the English and Scottish divi-

sions' of the majority. In view of the fact that we are

a small minority, and: that the never-ending story of

our persecution by’ the majority does not carry very

far, as‘the ear'of the majority is rather deaf to us,

whilst everything from Danny McCann downwards and

upwards is trumpeted ‘to the majority, the Ulster

Unionist“Council have acted very foolishly.  Truly,

they have 'given the show away.” They have, whilst

the English public were looking on, thrown a lurid light

on the‘tyranny, bigotry, and injustice of Unionism and

Unionists in Treland.

For generations the Irish Catholics have been a small

minority, and a badly-mauled minority at that, of the

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XXIII NO. 21, JANUARY 6 1912
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
sioner Yeats: indeed we enjoy hearing raucous cries of

““ bigot”’ shouted at us. We at least have the measure

of the Sourfaces, and are not afraid to publish the

fact.

The real fact is that Irish Catholics are a minority

of the working entity—Great Britain and Ireland. The

Treemasons in Ireland and such like have the ear of

Greab Britain. From Dan, Dan, Danny MecCann on-

wards they are playing into that ear. So vigorously

are they playing that we cannot even recount the re-

cent tunes. There was some alleged case in Cork, and

some fellow named Kingston or Kingstown was ex-

ported to do the gramaphone over in England. But

whatever little thing, real or alleged, happens that

may be represented against Irish Catholics, the majo-

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XXIV NO. 10, APRIL 20 1912
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
C.

It may be well to place a few extracts from Mr. As-

quith’s speech on record in these columns. He said i—

The Bill, therefore, proceeds in its first and

second Clauses to declare that after an appointed

day there shall be in Ireland an Irish Parliament,

consisting of his Majesty the King and two Houses,

namely, the Irish Senate and the Irish Iouse of

Commons. What are to be the legislative powers of

that Parliament? It is to have general power to

make lews for the peace, order and good govern-

ment of Ireland.

Again:—

Home Rule is, as we believe, a means of adjust-

ing Irish finance to Irish needs, of giving Irishmen

a direct interest in economy, which under

the partnership they have not gob, and a direct

responsibility for waste which under the partner-

smmense  demonstrations were written by him for the *‘Cork
Examiner,”’ and afterwards copied into the Dublin and English press.

0'Neill Daunt in his ““Ireland and her Agitators,” the last edition of

)

which appeared in 1888 as ‘85 years of Irish History,” pays a fine
sribute to Lenihan for his services as a political organiser and journalist.
The ‘‘Liberator”’ warmly admired him, and when. Lenihan founded the
« Tipperary Vindicator” at Nenagh in 1844, O’Connell wrote him a
very appreciative Tetter and for half a century this letter was on view
in his office in Limerick, to which city he removed in 1849 on taking

1)

over the ‘‘ Limerick Reporter,” a journal he had previously edited for
two years, 1841-43, when he accepted an engagement from John Francis
Maguire on the staff of tho ‘‘ Cork Examiner.”’ It was during Lenihan’s
editorship of the  Tipperary Vindicator ”’ that he exposed the famous
police plot known as ‘‘ The Shinrone Conspiracy,” and obtained the
dismissal of a large number of constables with their leader.

To ono of his marked hiterary gifts and antiquarian ta,q‘tes Limerick
